questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Sodium nitroprusside, $\mathrm{Na}_{2}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{5}\mathrm{NO}]$, is used as a reagent for the detection of a specific element in organic compounds during Lassaigne's test. Identify the element detected and the formula of the complex formed.
questions.solution:
In Lassaigne's test, sodium nitroprusside is used to detect the presence of sulphur in an organic compound.

When sodium extract containing sodium sulphide is treated with sodium nitroprusside, a purple or violet colour is produced due to the formation of sodium thionitroprusside.

The chemical reaction is given by:
$\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{S} + \mathrm{Na}_{2}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{5}\mathrm{NO}] \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{4}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{5}\mathrm{NOS}]$

Thus, the element detected is sulphur and the formula of the complex formed is $\mathrm{Na}_{4}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{5}\mathrm{NOS}]$.

Answer: Sulphur, $\mathrm{Na}_{4}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{5}\mathrm{NOS}]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Potassium hydroxide (KOH) can be used as a drying agent for:
questions.solution:
Potassium hydroxide (KOH) is a strong base. It reacts with acidic substances and compounds susceptible to base-catalyzed hydrolysis.

Esters undergo saponification (hydrolysis) in the presence of KOH.

Carboxylic acids react with KOH to form potassium carboxylate salts.

Phenols are acidic and react with KOH to form potassium phenoxide salts.

Amines are basic in nature and do not react with KOH. Therefore, solid KOH can be safely used as a drying agent for amines.

Answer: amines

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During Lassaigne's test, if both nitrogen and sulphur are present in an organic compound, they combine with sodium and carbon to form:
questions.solution:
During Lassaigne's test, when an organic compound containing both nitrogen and sulphur is fused with sodium metal, the sodium, carbon, nitrogen, and sulphur combine together to form sodium thiocyanate ($\mathrm{NaSCN}$).

The chemical equation for this fusion reaction is:
$\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{C} + \mathrm{N} + \mathrm{S} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaSCN}$

This compound gives a blood-red colouration with $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ ions due to the formation of the ferric thiocyanate complex.

Answer: $\mathrm{NaSCN}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will not give a positive Lassaigne's test for nitrogen?
questions.solution:
Lassaigne's test for nitrogen involves fusing the organic compound with sodium metal to form sodium cyanide ($\mathrm{NaCN}$).

$\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{C} + \mathrm{N} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{NaCN}$

For the formation of $\mathrm{NaCN}$, the compound must contain both carbon and nitrogen atoms.

Option (A) is hydrazine ($\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$), which contains nitrogen but lacks carbon. Therefore, it cannot form $\mathrm{NaCN}$ upon fusion with sodium and will not give a positive Lassaigne's test for nitrogen.

Options (B) sulfanilic acid, (C) phenylhydrazine, and (D) urea all contain both carbon and nitrogen, so they will form $\mathrm{NaCN}$ and give a positive test.

Answer: $\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following techniques is most appropriate for the separation of a mixture of acetone and carbon tetrachloride ($\mathrm{CCl}_{4}$)?
questions.solution:
The boiling point of acetone is $329\text{ K}$ ($56^\circ\text{C}$) and that of carbon tetrachloride is $350\text{ K}$ ($77^\circ\text{C}$).

The difference in their boiling points is approximately $21^\circ\text{C}$, which is less than $25^\circ\text{C}$.

For the separation of a mixture of two miscible liquids whose boiling points differ by less than $25^\circ\text{C}$, fractional distillation is the most appropriate technique.

Therefore, fractional distillation is used to separate a mixture of acetone and carbon tetrachloride.

Answer: Fractional distillation

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound is treated with $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and the resulting solution is then added to an alkaline solution of $\beta$-naphthol, producing a brilliant red dye. These observations indicate that the compound possesses a/an:
questions.solution:
Treatment of an organic compound with $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ at $0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ followed by the addition of an alkaline solution of $\beta$-naphthol to produce a brilliant red dye is the characteristic azo dye test.

Primary aromatic amines react with nitrous acid ($\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$) at low temperatures ($0^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to $5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$) to form stable arenediazonium salts.

The arenediazonium salt then undergoes an electrophilic substitution (coupling reaction) with the electron-rich aromatic ring of $\beta$-naphthol in an alkaline medium to form an intensely colored azo dye (brilliant red or orange-red).

Aliphatic primary amines form highly unstable diazonium salts that immediately decompose to yield nitrogen gas and alcohols, and thus do not give this test.

Therefore, the given observations indicate the presence of an aromatic $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group.

Answer: aromatic $-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ group

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the products A, B, and C in the given reaction sequence.
options: A = benzoic acid, B = sodium chloride, and C = isoquinoline
A = isoquinoline, B = benzoic acid, and C = sodium chloride
A = isoquinoline, B = sodium chloride, and C = benzoic acid
A = sodium chloride, B = isoquinoline, and C = benzoic acid
questions.solution:
The given starting material is an amide.

Step 1: Hydrolysis of the amide with $20\% \text{ NaOH}$ yields the corresponding free amine (1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline, referred to as isoquinoline in the options) and the sodium salt of the carboxylic acid (sodium benzoate).

Step 2: The reaction mixture is extracted with diethyl ether.
- The free amine (isoquinoline) is an organic compound and is soluble in the non-polar diethyl ether layer.
- The sodium benzoate is an ionic salt and remains in the aqueous layer.

Step 3: Evaporation of the ether layer leaves behind the amine. Therefore, A = isoquinoline.

Step 4: The aqueous layer containing sodium benzoate is acidified with $10\% \text{ HCl}$ to a pH of 2.
The acid-base reaction occurs as follows:
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COONa} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH} + \text{NaCl}$

Step 5: Benzoic acid is sparingly soluble in cold water and precipitates out of the solution. Therefore, the precipitate C = benzoic acid.
The sodium chloride formed remains dissolved in the aqueous solution. Therefore, the solution B = sodium chloride.

Thus, A = isoquinoline, B = sodium chloride, and C = benzoic acid.

Answer: A = isoquinoline, B = sodium chloride, and C = benzoic acid

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A mixture of phenol and benzoic acid can be separated using an aqueous solution of:
questions.solution:
Benzoic acid is a stronger acid than carbonic acid, whereas phenol is a weaker acid than carbonic acid.

Therefore, benzoic acid reacts with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ to form a water-soluble salt, sodium benzoate, with the evolution of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas.

Phenol does not react with aqueous $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ and remains insoluble.

This difference in reactivity allows the separation of phenol and benzoic acid using an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$.

Both phenol and benzoic acid react with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ to form water-soluble salts, so it cannot be used for separation.

$\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ is used as a qualitative test for phenol, not for separation.

Answer: $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will produce a blood red colour with $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ solution when its Lassaigne's extract is tested?
questions.solution:
The blood red colour with $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ solution in Lassaigne's test indicates the presence of both nitrogen and sulphur in the organic compound.

During sodium fusion, if both nitrogen and sulphur are present, sodium thiocyanate ($\mathrm{NaSCN}$) is formed.

$\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{C} + \mathrm{N} + \mathrm{S} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaSCN}$

When $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ is added to this extract, a blood red colouration is produced due to the formation of ferric thiocyanate.

$\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + \mathrm{SCN}^{-} \rightarrow [\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{SCN})]^{2+}$ (blood red)

Among the given options, only thiourea ($\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{CSNH}_{2}$) contains both nitrogen and sulphur.

Answer: $\mathrm{NH}_{2}\mathrm{CSNH}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The silver salt method is primarily used to determine the molecular weight of:
questions.solution:
The silver salt method is used to determine the equivalent weight and molecular weight of organic acids.

In this method, the organic acid is converted into its silver salt. A known mass of the silver salt is ignited to leave a residue of metallic silver. By knowing the mass of the silver salt and the mass of the silver residue, the equivalent weight of the silver salt and subsequently the organic acid can be calculated.

For organic bases, the chloroplatinate salt method is typically used.

Answer: organic acids

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In Lassaigne's test for the detection of nitrogen in an organic compound, the appearance of a Prussian blue colour is due to the formation of:
questions.solution:
In Lassaigne's test for nitrogen, the organic compound is fused with sodium metal to form sodium cyanide.

$\mathrm{Na} + \mathrm{C} + \mathrm{N} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCN}$

The sodium extract is boiled with iron(II) sulfate and then acidified with concentrated sulfuric acid. Iron(II) ions react with cyanide ions to form hexacyanoferrate(II) complex.

$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} + 6\mathrm{CN}^{-} \rightarrow [\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}]^{4-}$

Some iron(II) ions are oxidized to iron(III) ions upon heating with concentrated sulfuric acid. These iron(III) ions react with the hexacyanoferrate(II) complex to form iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II), which is Prussian blue in colour.

$4\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} + 3[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}]^{4-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{4}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}]_{3}$

The appearance of the Prussian blue colour is due to the formation of $\mathrm{Fe}_{4}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}]_{3}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{Fe}_{4}[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}]_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following extraction procedure for a mixture containing naphthalene and nicotine:

Identify the fraction where nicotine will be primarily recovered.
questions.solution:
The given mixture contains naphthalene and nicotine.

1. Naphthalene is a neutral, non-polar organic compound, whereas nicotine is a basic compound due to the presence of nitrogen atoms (amine groups).

2. When the mixture is dissolved in diethyl ether and treated with $10\%$ aqueous $\text{HCl}$, the basic nicotine reacts with the acid to form a water-soluble hydrochloride salt. This salt migrates to the aqueous layer.

3. Naphthalene does not react with $\text{HCl}$ and remains in the organic (ether) layer. Evaporation of this ether layer yields fraction (i), which is naphthalene.

4. The aqueous layer containing the nicotine salt is then made basic by adding $20\%$ aqueous $\text{NaOH}$. The strong base neutralizes the hydrochloride salt, regenerating the free base form of nicotine, which is insoluble in water but highly soluble in organic solvents.

5. Upon extraction with ether, the free nicotine moves into the new ether layer. Evaporation of this ether layer yields fraction (ii), which is nicotine.

6. The remaining aqueous layer contains inorganic salts (such as $\text{NaCl}$). Evaporation of this layer yields fraction (iii).

Therefore, nicotine is primarily recovered in fraction (ii).

Answer: Fraction (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following observations is correct and is commonly used in the identification of carboxylic acids?
options: Carboxylic acids produce a fruity smell of esters when heated with an alcohol in the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
Carboxylic acids give a positive iodoform test.
Carboxylic acids liberate $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gas from $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ solution.
questions.solution:
Carboxylic acids react with sodium bicarbonate ($\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$) to produce brisk effervescence due to the evolution of carbon dioxide ($\mathrm{CO}_{2}$) gas. This is a characteristic and widely used test for the identification of the carboxylic acid functional group.

The reaction is given by:
$\mathrm{RCOOH} + \mathrm{NaHCO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCOONa} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{CO}_{2} \uparrow$

While the esterification test (Option A) is also used, it is not universally applicable as higher molecular weight esters may not possess a distinct fruity smell. Option B is incorrect because carboxylic acids do not give a positive iodoform test. Therefore, the liberation of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ from $\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3}$ is the most common and reliable observation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the given separation scheme for a mixture of toluene, phenylacetic acid, and phenol:

Identify the correct final locations (X), (Y), and (Z) of these compounds.
options: Toluene is in (Z); phenylacetic acid is in (Y); phenol is in (X)
Toluene is in (Y); phenylacetic acid is in (X); phenol is in (Z)
Toluene is in (Z); phenylacetic acid is in (X); phenol is in (Y)
Toluene is in (Y); phenylacetic acid is in (Y); phenol is in (Z)
questions.solution:
The given mixture contains toluene (a neutral hydrocarbon), phenylacetic acid (a carboxylic acid, $pKa = 4.3$), and phenol (a weak acid, $pKa = 10$).

When the mixture is treated with aqueous $NaHCO_3$ (a weak base) and ether, only the stronger acid, phenylacetic acid, reacts to form water-soluble sodium phenylacetate. This moves into the aqueous layer. Phenol is a weaker acid than carbonic acid and does not react with $NaHCO_3$, so it remains in the ether layer along with the neutral toluene.

The aqueous layer containing sodium phenylacetate is then treated with aqueous $HCl$. This strong acid protonates the salt back into phenylacetic acid, which is then extracted into Ether (X). Therefore, (X) contains phenylacetic acid.

The ether layer containing phenol and toluene is treated with aqueous $NaOH$ (a strong base). Phenol reacts with $NaOH$ to form water-soluble sodium phenoxide, which moves into the aqueous layer. Toluene does not react and remains in the ether layer. Therefore, Ether (Z) contains toluene.

The aqueous layer containing sodium phenoxide is treated with aqueous $HCl$, which protonates the salt back into phenol. This phenol is then extracted into Ether (Y). Therefore, (Y) contains phenol.

Thus, (X) is phenylacetic acid, (Y) is phenol, and (Z) is toluene.

Answer: Toluene is in (Z); phenylacetic acid is in (X); phenol is in (Y)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will most readily produce a precipitate when treated with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$?
questions.solution:
The formation of a precipitate with an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ depends on the ease of ionization of the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond to form a stable carbocation and a bromide ion ($\mathrm{Br}^{-}$).

Let us analyze the carbocations formed by the heterolytic cleavage of the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond in each given compound:

For option (B), bromobenzene: Cleavage of the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond produces a phenyl cation. The positive charge is on an $sp^2$ hybridized carbon, making it highly unstable. Additionally, the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond has partial double bond character due to resonance, making it difficult to break.

For option (C), 7-bromo-1,3,5-cycloheptatriene: Cleavage of the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond produces the cycloheptatrienyl (tropylium) cation. This cation is cyclic, planar, fully conjugated, and contains $6\pi$ electrons. According to Hückel's rule ($4n+2$ $\pi$ electrons, with $n=1$), it is aromatic. Because aromatic carbocations are exceptionally stable, this compound will readily ionize to give $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$, which then reacts with $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ to form a precipitate of $\mathrm{AgBr}$.

For option (D), vinyl bromide: Cleavage of the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond produces a vinyl cation, which is highly unstable as the positive charge is on an $sp$ hybridized carbon. Like bromobenzene, the $\mathrm{C-Br}$ bond also has partial double bond character.

Therefore, 7-bromo-1,3,5-cycloheptatriene will most readily produce a precipitate.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When phenol is fused with phthalic anhydride in the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, a compound B is formed. What is the colour of compound B in an alkaline medium?
questions.solution:
When phenol is fused with phthalic anhydride in the presence of concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, a condensation reaction takes place to form phenolphthalein, which is compound B.

Phenolphthalein is a well-known acid-base indicator.

In an acidic or neutral medium, phenolphthalein exists in its unionised form and is colourless.

In an alkaline medium, it loses protons to form a highly conjugated dianion, which imparts a characteristic pink colour to the solution.

Therefore, the colour of compound B in an alkaline medium is pink.

Answer: Pink

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Disodium pentacyanonitrosylferrate reagent gives a purple colour when which of the following elements is present?
questions.solution:
Disodium pentacyanonitrosylferrate is commonly known as sodium nitroprusside, $\text{Na}_2[\text{Fe(CN)}_5\text{NO}]$.

In Lassaigne's test for the detection of elements in organic compounds, the sodium fusion extract containing sulphide ions ($\text{S}^{2-}$) reacts with sodium nitroprusside to give a purple or violet colour.

The reaction is:

$\text{S}^{2-} + [\text{Fe(CN)}_5\text{NO}]^{2-} \rightarrow [\text{Fe(CN)}_5\text{NOS}]^{4-}$

The purple colour is due to the formation of the $[\text{Fe(CN)}_5\text{NOS}]^{4-}$ complex.

Therefore, this reagent is used to detect the presence of sulphur (S).

Answer: S

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Rectified spirit is a mixture containing approximately:
questions.solution:
Rectified spirit is a highly concentrated ethanol solution purified by repeated distillation. It forms an azeotropic mixture containing approximately $95.6\%$ ethanol and $4.4\%$ water by mass (or volume).

Because it is an azeotrope, it boils at a constant temperature and cannot be further purified into absolute ($100\%$) ethanol by simple fractional distillation.

Answer: 95.6% ethanol and 4.4% water

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is incorrect?
options: The compound will not respond to the haloform test.
Fehling's solution is a suitable reagent for the detection of aromatic aldehydes.
Schiff's reagent and Schiff's base are different compounds.
Both aldehydes and ketones can react with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine reagent.
questions.solution:
Let us evaluate each statement:

(A) The given compound is acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$). The haloform test is given by compounds containing a methyl ketone group ($\text{CH}_3\text{CO}-$ attached to a carbon or hydrogen atom) or those that can be oxidized to it. Carboxylic acids and their derivatives do not give the haloform test. Thus, this statement is correct.

(B) Fehling's solution is a weak oxidizing agent. It can oxidize aliphatic aldehydes but is not strong enough to oxidize aromatic aldehydes (such as benzaldehyde). Therefore, Fehling's solution is not a suitable reagent for the detection of aromatic aldehydes. Thus, this statement is incorrect.

(C) Schiff's reagent is a dye (rosaniline hydrochloride decolorized by sulfur dioxide) used to test for aldehydes. A Schiff's base is an imine ($\text{R}-\text{N}=\text{CR}'_2$) formed by the condensation of a primary amine with an aldehyde or ketone. They are entirely different compounds. Thus, this statement is correct.

(D) Both aldehydes and ketones contain a carbonyl group and react with 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine (Brady's reagent) to form yellow, orange, or red precipitates of 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazones. Thus, this statement is correct.

Therefore, the incorrect statement is (B).

Answer: Fehling's solution is a suitable reagent for the detection of aromatic aldehydes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An organic compound contains $\mathrm{C}$, $\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{N}$, $\mathrm{S}$, and $\mathrm{Cl}$. For the detection of chlorine, the sodium extract of the compound is first heated with a few drops of concentrated $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ and then $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added to obtain a white precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$. The purpose of digestion with $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ before the addition of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is:
options: to prevent the hydrolysis of $\mathrm{NaCN}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{S}$
to create a common ion effect

to convert $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ to volatile $\mathrm{HCN}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S}$, otherwise they will interfere with the test by forming $\mathrm{AgCN}$ or $\mathrm{Ag}_{2}\mathrm{S}$
questions.solution:
In Lassaigne's test for halogens, if nitrogen and sulphur are also present in the organic compound, the sodium extract will contain $\mathrm{NaCN}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{S}$ along with $\mathrm{NaX}$ (where $\mathrm{X}$ is the halogen).

If $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added directly, $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ ions will react to form precipitates of $\mathrm{AgCN}$ (white) and $\mathrm{Ag}_{2}\mathrm{S}$ (black), which interfere with the detection of the halogen.

To prevent this, the sodium extract is first boiled with concentrated $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$. This decomposes $\mathrm{NaCN}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{S}$ into volatile $\mathrm{HCN}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S}$ gases, which escape from the solution.

$\mathrm{NaCN} + \mathrm{HNO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaNO}_{3} + \mathrm{HCN} \uparrow$

$\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{S} + 2\mathrm{HNO}_{3} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{NaNO}_{3} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S} \uparrow$

After these interfering ions are removed, $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ is added to detect the chloride ion as a white precipitate of $\mathrm{AgCl}$.

Answer: to convert $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ to volatile $\mathrm{HCN}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{S}$, otherwise they will interfere with the test by forming $\mathrm{AgCN}$ or $\mathrm{Ag}_{2}\mathrm{S}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A mixture of 4-nitrotoluene, p-cresol, and p-toluidine is dissolved in ether and subjected to the following separation scheme:



Identify the products (i), (ii), and (iii).
questions.solution:
The mixture contains 4-nitrotoluene (neutral), p-cresol (acidic), and p-toluidine (basic).

When the ether solution of the mixture is treated with dilute HCl, the basic component, p-toluidine, reacts to form a water-soluble salt (p-toluidinium chloride) and moves into the aqueous layer. The neutral 4-nitrotoluene and acidic p-cresol remain in the ether layer.

The aqueous HCl layer is then treated with dilute NaOH, which neutralizes the salt and regenerates the free base, p-toluidine. This forms precipitate (iii).

The remaining ether layer is treated with dilute NaOH. The acidic component, p-cresol, reacts to form a water-soluble salt (sodium p-cresolate) and moves into the aqueous layer. The neutral 4-nitrotoluene remains in the ether layer.

The aqueous NaOH layer is treated with dilute HCl, which neutralizes the salt and regenerates the free phenol, p-cresol. This forms precipitate (i).

Finally, the remaining ether layer is evaporated to yield the neutral component, 4-nitrotoluene. This forms precipitate (ii).

Therefore, (i) is p-cresol, (ii) is 4-nitrotoluene, and (iii) is p-toluidine.

Answer: (i) p-cresol, (ii) 4-nitrotoluene, (iii) p-toluidine

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following methods is used to separate a mixture of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines?
questions.solution:
Hinsberg's method is used to separate and distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary amines.

In this method, the amine mixture is treated with benzenesulfonyl chloride (Hinsberg's reagent).

Primary amines react to form an N-alkylbenzenesulfonamide, which is soluble in aqueous alkali.

Secondary amines react to form an N,N-dialkylbenzenesulfonamide, which is insoluble in aqueous alkali.

Tertiary amines do not react with benzenesulfonyl chloride and remain insoluble in alkali but dissolve in acids.

Answer: Hinsberg's method

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Phenol can be distinguished from ethanol by the use of:
questions.solution:
Phenol reacts with neutral $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ solution to form a violet-coloured water-soluble complex, which indicates the presence of a phenolic $-\mathrm{OH}$ group.

Ethanol, being an aliphatic alcohol, does not react with neutral $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ solution and produces no colour change.

Benedict's reagent, Schiff's reagent, and Tollen's reagent are primarily used to test for aldehydes and do not give characteristic tests with either phenol or ethanol.

Answer: $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents can be used to chemically distinguish between $p$-chloroaniline ($\mathrm{p}\text{-}\mathrm{Cl}\text{-}\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$) and anilinium chloride ($\mathrm{PhNH}_{3}^{+}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$)?
questions.solution:
Anilinium chloride ($\mathrm{PhNH}_{3}^{+}\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$) is an ionic salt containing a free chloride ion ($\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$). When treated with aqueous $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, it undergoes a precipitation reaction to form a white precipitate of silver chloride ($\mathrm{AgCl}$).

$\mathrm{PhNH}_{3}^{+}\mathrm{Cl}^{-} + \mathrm{AgNO}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}\downarrow + \mathrm{PhNH}_{3}^{+}\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}$

In contrast, $p$-chloroaniline ($p\text{-}\mathrm{Cl}\text{-}\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{4}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$) contains a chlorine atom that is covalently bonded to the benzene ring. Due to the partial double bond character arising from resonance, this $\mathrm{C}\text{-}\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is strong and does not ionize in solution. Therefore, it does not react with $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ to give a precipitate.

Thus, $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$ can be used to chemically distinguish between the two compounds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following techniques is most suitable for the purification of anthracene?
questions.solution:
Anthracene is a solid substance that undergoes sublimation, meaning it transitions directly from the solid phase to the gas phase upon heating without passing through the liquid phase.

Impurities present in anthracene generally do not sublime. Therefore, when the impure mixture is heated, anthracene vaporizes and can be collected on a cool surface as a pure solid, leaving the non-sublimable impurities behind.

Thus, sublimation is the most suitable technique for the purification of anthracene.

Answer: Sublimation
